title: The Birdï؟½s Nest. A Tale.[1]
content:
آ آ آ آ In Scotlandï؟½s realms, where trees are few,
آ آ آ آ Nor even shrubs abound;
آ آ آ آ But where, however bleak the view,
آ آ آ آ Some better things are found;


آ آ آ آ For husband there and wife may boast
آ آ آ آ There union undefiled,
آ آ آ آ And false ones are as rare almost
آ آ آ آ As hedgerows in the wildï؟½


آ آ آ آ In Scotlandï؟½s realm forlorn and bare
آ آ آ آ The history chanced of lateï؟½
آ آ آ آ The history of a wedded pair,
آ آ آ آ A chaffinch and his mate.


آ آ آ آ The spring drew near, each felt a breast
آ آ آ آ With genial instinct fillï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ They pairï؟½d, and would have built a nest,
آ آ آ آ But found not where to build.


آ آ آ آ The heaths uncoverï؟½d and the moors
آ آ آ آ Except with snow and sleet,
آ آ آ آ Sea-beaten rocks and naked shores
آ آ آ آ Could yield them no retreat.


آ آ آ آ Long time a breeding-place they sought,
آ آ آ آ Till both grew vexï؟½d and tired;
آ آ آ آ At length a ship arriving brought
آ آ آ آ The good so long desired.


آ آ آ آ A ship!ï؟½could such a restless thing
آ آ آ آ Afford them place of rest?
آ آ آ آ Or was the merchant charged to bring
آ آ آ آ The homeless birds a nest?


آ آ آ آ Hushï؟½silent hearers profit mostï؟½
آ آ آ آ This racer of the sea
آ آ آ آ Proved kinder to them than the coast,
آ آ آ آ It served them with a tree.


آ آ آ آ But such a tree! ï؟½twas shaven deal,
آ آ آ آ The tree they call a mast,
آ آ آ آ And had a hollow with a wheel
آ آ آ آ Through which the tackle passï؟½d.


آ آ آ آ Within that cavity aloft
آ آ آ آ Their roofless home they fixï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Formï؟½d with materials neat and soft,
آ آ آ آ Bents, wool, and feathers mixï؟½d.


آ آ آ آ Four ivory eggs soon pave its floor
آ آ آ آ With russet specks bedightï؟½
آ آ آ آ The vessel weighs, forsakes the shore,
آ آ آ آ And lessens to the sight.


آ آ آ آ The mother-bird is gone to sea,
آ آ آ آ As she had changed her kind;
آ آ آ آ But goes the male? Far wiser, he
آ آ آ آ Is doubtless left behind.


آ آ آ آ Noï؟½soon as from ashore he saw
آ آ آ آ The winged mansion move,
آ آ آ آ He flew to reach it, by a law
آ آ آ آ Of never-failing love;


آ آ آ آ Then, perching at his consortï؟½s side,
آ آ آ آ Was briskly borne along,
آ آ آ آ The billows and the blast defied,
آ آ آ آ And cheerï؟½d her with a song.


آ آ آ آ The seaman with sincere delight
آ آ آ آ His featherï؟½d shipmates eyes,
آ آ آ آ Scarce less exulting in the sight
آ آ آ آ Than when he tows a prize.


آ آ آ آ For seamen much believe in signs,
آ آ آ آ And from a chance so new
آ آ آ آ Each some approaching good divines,
آ آ آ آ And may his hopes be true!


آ آ آ آ Hail, honourï؟½d land! a desert where
آ آ آ آ Not even birds can hide,
آ آ آ آ Yet parent of this loving pair
آ آ آ آ Whom nothing could divide.


آ آ آ آ And ye who, rather than resign
آ آ آ آ Your matrimonial plan,
آ آ آ آ Were not afraid to plough the brine
آ آ آ آ In company with man;


آ آ آ آ For whose lean country much disdain
آ آ آ آ We English often show,
آ آ آ آ Yet from a richer nothing gain
آ آ آ آ But wantonness and woeï؟½


آ آ آ آ Be it your fortune, year by year
آ آ آ آ The same resource to prove,
آ آ آ آ And may ye, sometimes landing here,
آ آ آ آ Instruct us how to love!
meaning:
This evocative piece by William Cowper, titled "The Birdï؟½s Nest. A Tale.[1]", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
